seoTitle: Adjust Accounts Payable With a Journal Entry
metaDescription: Correct a vendor liability balance in QuickBooks Online using a journal entry that properly links to the vendor's sub-ledger.
intro: Adjusting Accounts Payable isn't quite like a normal journal entry, since it needs to stay linked to a specific vendor to keep sub-ledgers accurate. This guide shows how to write off or correct a vendor balance in QuickBooks Online the right way.

steps.heading: Open a new journal entry
steps.body: Click + New, select Journal Entry, and set the adjustment date with a unique journal number.

steps.heading: Select Accounts Payable
steps.body: In the first row, choose Accounts Payable (A/P) as the account and place the amount under Debit to reduce the balance or Credit to increase it.

steps.heading: Link the vendor
steps.body: In the same row, select the target vendor's name in the Name column so QuickBooks preserves the sub-ledger link.

steps.heading: Add the offsetting line
steps.body: On the second row, choose an offsetting account, such as an expense or equity account, to balance the entry.

steps.heading: Save and verify
steps.body: Add a memo for context, save the entry, then run an A/P Aging Summary report to confirm the vendor's balance adjusted correctly.

faq.question: Why do I need to select a vendor name on an A/P journal entry?
faq.answer: Accounts Payable is a sub-ledger account, so linking a vendor keeps the adjustment tied to their specific balance instead of leaving it unassigned.

faq.question: Can I use this to write off an uncollectible vendor balance?
faq.answer: Yes, this method is commonly used to write off or correct baseline vendor balances that no longer reflect reality.

faq.question: What account should I use to offset the A/P line?
faq.answer: It depends on the reason for the adjustment; common choices include an expense account or opening balance equity.

faq.question: How do I confirm the adjustment worked?
faq.answer: Run an Accounts Payable Aging Summary report to verify the target vendor's open balance reflects the change.
